observations.recommendation:
13. When no action was taken, a second demand letter was sent on March 31, 2025. In line with Section 122 of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445, we recommended to the Local Chief Executive (LCE) the suspension of the Municipal Accountant’s salary due to continued non-compliance. We also reminded Management that under Section 127 of the same Decree, any unjustified failure of a public officer to comply with the requirements of the Code constitutes neglect of duty, which may warrant administrative disciplinary action. If found guilty after due process, the responsible officer shall be penalized in accordance with the Civil Service Law.

17. We recommended and the MTO agreed to establish a strict internal schedule for the timely submission of all accounting source documents to avoid delays in the preparation of the year-end FS.

18. We also recommended and the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) agreed to prioritize the prompt hiring and deployment of qualified personnel to fill the vacant positions in the accounting office, subject to the lifting of the COMELEC election ban, to ensure continuity and efficiency in financial reporting activities.

19. We further recommended that the LCE conduct a formal assessment to determine accountability among all involved offices for the delayed submission of the FS for CY 2024, ensuring a fair and objective evaluation under Sections 122 and 127 of PD No. 1445, and help identify systemic improvements to prevent future delays.

22. The Audit Team (AT) stressed that the two-month lag in DV transmittal from the Treasurer’s Office greatly impacts the Accountant’s reporting. While recognizing the Accountant’s efforts, the AT reiterated that she remains the final processor of financial reports. To address the issue, the AT recommended that all communications be documented and a memo be issued to reiterate deadlines and responsibilities. Stronger coordination among offices and active oversight by department heads were also advised to prevent future delays.

28. We recommended and the Municipal Health Officer (MHO) agreed to require all recipients of the HEA to execute a quitclaim, which shall include an undertaking to return any excess amount received in case of duplicate payments for the same month, in accordance with the provisions of the MOA.

29. We further recommended and the MHO agreed to fully comply with the provisions of the MOA entered into with the DOH-CVCHD for the payment of HEA benefits.

36. We recommend and the members of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) agreed to strictly comply with the procurement procedures prescribed under RA 9184 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations, and ensure that all future procurement activities are undertaken through competitive bidding or duly approved alternative methods. Reimbursements should be limited to exceptional and duly justified cases supported by complete documentation and prior authorization from the Head of the Procuring Entity.

43. We recommended and Management agreed to strictly ensure that cash advances with a total cash accountability of ₱2,000.00 or more, excluding those related to official travel, are granted only to personnel who are duly bonded, in compliance with Section 101 of PD No. 1445 and Item 7.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

51. We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to ensure the timely preparation and submission of the Semestral Report on the Status of Unliquidated Cash Advances, Fund Transfers, and Other Receivables on or before the 8th day following the end of each semester, in compliance with Section 122 of P.D. 1445 and COA Memorandum 2017-010 dated May 15, 2017.

1. We recommended that Management submit a list of employees whose achievements resulted in monetary savings, indicating the amount of monetary savings generated by their suggestions, inventions, superior accomplishments, and other personal efforts. Henceforth, adhere strictly to the provisions of CSC MC No. 1, series of 2001 in the granting of monetary awards under the PRAISE.
observations.managementAction: Management had not submitted a list of employees whose special achievements resulted in monetary savings, indicating the amount of monetary savings generated by their suggestions, inventions, superior accomplishments, and other personal efforts. Henceforth, adhere strictly to the provisions of CSC MC No. 1, series of 2001 in the granting of monetary awards under the PRAISE.

observations.recommendation: 5. We recommended that Management fill up the positions for the mandatory positions in compliance with NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1 to ensure the implementation of actions and measures related to disaster risk reduction and management.
observations.managementAction: The two mandatory positions in LDRRMO still had not been filled up as of date.

observations.recommendation: 6. We recommended that Management: (a) require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the cashbook balances to determine the valid reconciling items for adjustments in the books of accounts; (b) require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the adjusting entry debiting Cash in Bank- Local Currency, Current Account (1-01-02-010) and crediting Cash- Local Treasury (1-01-01-010) amounting to ₱11,000,000.00 based on the bank credit memo to recognize the deposit of the fund transfer; and (c) require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to, henceforth, conduct regular reconciliation of the cashbooks with the subsidiary accounting records in accordance with Sections 63(A.2) and 63.F of the Local Treasury Operations Manual and pertinent provisions of the NGAS Manual.
observations.managementAction:
The incumbent Municipal Treasurer had just assumed her position early this year, thus, still had reconcile her records with the accounting records.
a. The reconciliation of the cashbook balance with the subsidiary accounting records was not complied with with; thus, we could not ascertain both records’ accuracy, completeness, and correctness.
b. The Local Government Support Fund (LGSF) from the Department of Budget and Management (DBM) directly credited to the Municipality’s bank account amounting to ₱11,000,000.00 was erroneously recorded as Cash Local Treasury (1-01-01-010), thereby overstating the account by the same amount.

observations.recommendation:
7. We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer furnish the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the year with a certified list of taxpayers with the amount due and collectible for the current year as their basis for recording the RPT and SET Receivables in compliance with Sections 19(b) and 20, Volume I of the NGAS Manual.
8. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the adjusting entry debiting Cash in Bank-Local Currency, Current Account (1-01-02-010) and crediting Due to NGAs (2-02-01-050) amounting to ₱11,000,000.00.
9. We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to update and maintain the subsidiary ledgers of the general ledger accounts, conduct a thorough review of the affected accounts to determine the other causes of the abnormal balances, initiate corrections/adjustments, and thereafter come up with all accounts in their normal balances to fairly present the financial statements.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Treasurer had not furnished the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the year with the certified list of Taxpayers with the amount due and collectible for the current year.
The incumbent Municipal Treasurer did not know about the subject transaction.
The Municipal Accountant had not submitted the JEV to support the adjustment made on the erroneous recording.

observations.recommendation:
10. We recommended that Management require the elected officials who are not among those entitled to Hazard Pay as provided under Section 2 of A.O. No. 26 and Section 3 of DBM Budget Circular No. 2020-1 dated March 24, 2020, to refund the amount of ₱126,000.00.
11. We further recommended that Management use solely for COVID-19 response operations the financial assistance given by the Provincial Government, otherwise, return the unexpended balance to the Provincial Government of Negros Oriental to be utilized for other equally important government programs and projects.
12. We further recommended that Management, henceforth, ensure that the officials involved in the procurement process strictly comply with the procedures for Negotiated Procurement through SVP under Item 8.b, Annex H of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184, to protect the interest of the government.
observations.managementAction:
The persons liable still had not refunded the amount.
However, the Audit Team had not been provided with the records of the number of clients the health office accommodates daily to assess the necessity of the health consultant.

observations.recommendation:
13. We recommended that in the preparation of the Annual Budget, the Head of the OSCA submit to the Municipal Budget Officer a request for allotment of funds for the maintenance and operation of the OSCA showing the estimated amount needed for each function, activity, or purpose consistent with the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs, as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.
14. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the abovementioned documentary requirements provided under Section 3.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to support the fund transfers or financial assistance extended to OSCA totaling ₱120,000.00.
observations.managementAction: Management still had not submitted the supporting documents mentioned above to the COA Auditor.

observations.recommendation:
15. We recommended that Management submit the following: a. An explanation of the issuance of work orders or contracts and the delivery of services by the supplier before the procurement procedures provided under Item 8.b, Annex H of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 were undertaken; b. An explanation of why the caterers who participated in the canvass were able to come up with their bids despite the lack of a menu; and c. A breakdown of each caterer’s menu offering.
16. We further recommended that, henceforth, Management ensure that the officials involved in the procurement process strictly comply with the procedures for Negotiated Procurement through SVP under Item 8.b, Annex H of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184, to protect the interest of the government.
observations.managementAction:
Management said that the necessity of the consultant was valid due to the number of clients their MHO handles.
However, the Audit Team had not been provided with the records of the number of clients the health office accommodates daily to assess the necessity of the health consultant.

observations.recommendation:
17. We recommended that Management refrain from hiring consultants whose functions can be performed by regularly appointed officials of existing offices of the LGU as set forth in laws, rules and regulations or in their position description form to avoid the unnecessary duplication of works/functions and wastage of government funds.
18. We further recommended that, in the hiring of consultants, Management should clearly define the consultants’ expected output in their individual contract and require the latter to prepare their monthly accomplishment reports vis-à-vis their respective definite expected output to establish the necessity of the consultancy services they are employed for. Henceforth, payment of the services rendered by medical consultants shall be properly supported with the documentary requirements provided under Section 9.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.
observations.managementAction: However, the Audit Team had not been provided with the records of the number of clients the health office accommodates daily to assess the necessity of the health consultant.

observations.recommendation:
19. We recommended that Management submit a list of employees with special achievements recognized by an outside group, indicating the amount of monetary savings generated by their suggestions, inventions, superior accomplishments, and other personal efforts in accordance with Section 6 of CSC MC No. 1, s. 2001.
20. We further recommended that Management discontinue the use of their savings for payment of monetary awards under the PRAISE program in the absence of proof to establish that these savings were generated from employees’ suggestions, innovations, inventions, superior accomplishments, and other personal efforts. Otherwise, they could be held liable for willful violations of existing laws, rules, and regulations.
observations.managementAction: Management ignored the Audit Team’s recommendations and granted PRAISE monetary incentive in CY 2022.

observations.recommendation:
21. We recommended that Management require the Inventory Committee to prepare and submit the duly accomplished RPCI.
22. Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjustments, to reflect the correct balances of the Inventory accounts in the financial statements based on the result of the inventory count of the Inventory Committee.
23. Henceforth, we recommended that Management properly record the purchases of inventories in the appropriate inventory accounts and the consumption thereof as expense, upon receipt of the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) from the Property Officer.
observations.managementAction: The inventory items had still been directly charged to expense.

Henceforth, strict compliance to COA Circular No. 2005-002 dated April 14, 2005, and Section 4(ee) of COA Circular No. 2020-004 dated January 31, 2020, is enjoined.
observations.managementAction: Unimplemented

27. We recommended that Management require the BAC, through its Secretariat, to post in the GPPB online portal the updated APP, NOA, and other information relative to the procurement of projects in accordance with the provisions of GPPB Circular No. 01- 2020 dated April 6, 2020.
28. We also recommended that the LCE send a written notice to the Auditor and other government agencies stated under Section 4.4 of DBM LBC No. 125 dated April 7, 2020, on the posting of NOA, Contract or Purchase Orders and other necessary information in the GPPB Online Portal.
observations.managementAction:
The BAC, through its Secretariat had not posted in the GPPB online portal the updated APP, NOA, and other information relative to the procurement of projects in accordance with the provisions of GPPB Circular No. 01- 2020 dated April 6, 2020.
The LCE had not sent a written notice to the Auditor and other government agencies stated under Section 4.4 of DBM LBC No. 125 dated April 7, 2020, on the posting of NOA, Contract or Purchase Orders and other necessary information in the GPPB Online Portal.

observations.recommendation:

31. We recommended further and Management agreed to submit Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption to the Auditor for verification purposes pursuant to the Manual on Audit for Fuel Consumption of Motor Vehicles (COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977).
observations.managementAction:
Our verification of the trip tickets disclosed that still, these had not been completely filled out. Likewise, the Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption had not been submitted to the COA Auditor for verification purposes.
Management had not submitted the Monthly Report on Official Travels and Monthly Report on Fuel Consumption to the Auditor for verification purposes.

observations.recommendation:
32. We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) submit copies of the Mayor’s/Business Permit and duly notarized OSS. observations.managementAction: The legal documents for the procurement of fuel still had to be submitted or attached in the DVs. Further, the Audit Team had not received an explanation for the withdrawal of 5,250.20 liters of diesel before the PR was approved. Likewise, the BAC and other officials who approved the award still had not submitted their explanations to the Audit Team.

Lastly, we recommended that BAC and other officials who approved the award and payment to submit their explanations why they should not be held liable for the loss to the government of at least ₱375,000.00 arising from the purchase of rice from private suppliers at higher prices than that offered by NFA.
observations.managementAction:
Management had not submitted an MOA with NFA to the Audit Team.
BAC and other officials still had not submitted their explanations to the Audit Team.

observations.managementAction: The distribution sheets did not provide the quantity per unit of relief goods distributed to the constituents, rendering it difficult to account for the quantity distributed and the remaining inventory.

We recommended that Management refrain from charging the aforementioned expenditures to the LDRRMF and, henceforth, utilize it for PPAs enumerated under NDRRMC, DBM, and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 dated March 25, 2013, to strengthen the capacity of the Municipality to avoid or mitigate the adverse effects of disasters or calamity.

observations.managementAction: Unimplemented

observations.managementAction: Unimplemented

observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant had not submitted the JEV to support the adjustment made on the erroneous entry.

49. observations.managementAction: The LDRRMO had not submitted the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF for the months of January to June 2019 to the Auditor.

observations.managementAction: The subject stock cards had not been submitted to the Auditor for verification.

observations.recommendation: 67. We recommended that Management strictly observe the regulations on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances pursuant to COA Circular No 97-002 dated February 10, 1997. 68. We further recommended that management demand from concerned employees for the immediate settlement of their cash advances.
observations.managementAction: Cash advances that have been outstanding for over one year and above had not been demanded for immediate settlement from concerned employees.

observations.recommendation: 69. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant see to it that: (a) the grant of initial cash advance for the PCF is supported with authority by the Local Chief Executive and estimate of expenses; (b) on the grant of initial replenishment of the cash advance is made only when the disbursements reach at least 75%, or as the need requires, by submitting a replenishment voucher supported by PCVs and other documents duly summarized in a report of disbursements; and (c) the PCF is not used for regular expenses, such as rentals, subscription, utilities and the like.
observations.managementAction: Cash advances had been replenished when disbursements reached 70% of the petty cash. However, the regulations had not been strictly followed.

observations.managementAction: The LCPC in the Municipality had not submitted the Records or Minutes of Meetings and Accomplishment Report, hence, the Audit Team could not review and validate whether it is fully operational.

observations.recommendation:
71. We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require the Disbursing Officer to submit the lacking documents and henceforth, strictly comply with the documentary requirements enumerated under Section 1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-01 dated June 14, 2012.
72. We further recommended that management demand from concerned employees for the immediate settlement of their cash advances.
observations.managementAction: Cash advances had not been supported by the required documents. The supporting documents for the subject transactions had not been submitted to the Auditor.

observations.recommendation: 73. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant see to it that: (a) the grant of initial cash advance for the PCF is supported with authority by the Local Chief Executive and estimate of expenses; (b) on the grant of initial replenishment of the cash advance is made only when the disbursements reach at least 75%, or as the need requires, by submitting a replenishment voucher supported by PCVs and other documents duly summarized in a report of disbursements; and (c) the PCF is not used for regular expenses, such as rentals, subscription, utilities and the like.

observations.managementAction: Management had not submitted an explanation of the negative balances of the “Due to BIR” which indicates erroneous recording and remittance of the taxes.

observations.recommendation: 75. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, together with the Sanggunian Bayan, require a refund of the excess payment and discontinue the granting of excess of the allowable maximum rate of ₱5,000.00 per personnel, as provided for in Section 1.a of E.O. No. 80 dated July 20, 2012, to avoid overpayment.
observations.managementAction:
Management had not provided proof of payment.
No refund had been made.

76. We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer desist from making advance payments in accordance with Item 4, Annex D of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.
77. The Property and Inspection Officer must submit a written explanation on the discrepancy noted between the date the Acceptance and Inspection Report was signed by them and the actual dates of delivery of the materials per delivery receipts.

79. We recommended immediate refund in full of the amounts received in excess of what is authorized in DBM Budget Circular No. 2010-3 totaling ₱748,000.00.

observations.managementAction: Management had not taken action to refund.

The Municipal Treasurer prepare the IIRUP with the appraisal of each item made by an appraisal committee for the purpose and have those items inspected and approved by the Regional Technical and Information Technology Services, COA Region VII. c. The Municipal Treasurer undertake the disposal of the unserviceable properties following the procedures and guidelines provided under Sections 502-505 of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual, Volume I. d. Management required the Office of the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to drop the amounts of the unserviceable property from their books after disposal, and the municipal accountant to record the proceeds of the auction sale, if any.

100. We recommended that Management require the Assessor, as well as the Municipal Treasurer to convene the Provincial Appraisal Committee to immediately act in the determination of the value of the unrecorded lots. Also direct them to process the titling of the lots owned by the Municipality and for the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate funds for the titling thereof.
101. Likewise, instruct the Municipal Accountant to review the land account and record only in the books of accounts those lots that are owned by the municipality. Moreover, the value of the lots which will be determined by the Provincial Appraisal Committee shall also be recorded in the books of accounts to avoid understatement of assets.
observations.managementAction:

observations.recommendation:
1.11 We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to:
a. Assist the Municipal Accountant in reconciling the difference of ₱111,615,997.53 between the accounting and property records and the result of the actual physical count;
b. Adopt the uniform numbering system for property numbers of PPE and require the Property Unit to update the property stickers based on the prescribed format and to include the required information under Section 5.6 and 5.7 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020; and

2.17 We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Create an Inventory Committee to focus on the physical count of local roads; and
b. Enjoin the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Accountant, and Municipal Engineer to strictly comply with the accounting and reporting guidelines on the local roads asset management system pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated October 23, 2015.

3.6 We recommended and the:
a. Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to maintain PPE Ledger Cards to track all the transactions related to PPE.
b. Municipal Accountant agreed ensure that all depreciable PPEs have been provided with a depreciation expense and that all necessary data have been appropriately recorded in compliance with IPSAS 17.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal
Accountant to reconcile their records with the bank statements to determine the
valid reconciling items for adjustments in the books of accounts and submit the
overdue BRS for the prior years up to December 31, 2021, together with copies
of the bank statements, subsidiary ledgers of each depository account, and
original copies of the debit and credit memos.
We further recommended that, henceforth, Management ensure that the
Municipal Accountant complies with the deadline for the preparation and
submission of the BRS to the Auditor for verification and timely review thereof,
otherwise, suspend the salaries of the Municipal Accountant and concerned
responsible employees for the undue delay in the submission of the BRS until
they shall have complied with the requirements of the Commission in pursuance
of Section 122 of P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended and Management agreed to: (a) require the Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the cashbook balances to
determine the valid reconciling items for adjustments in the books of accounts;
(b) require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the adjusting entry debiting
Cash in Bank-Local Currency, Current Account (1-01-02-010) and crediting
Cash- Local Treasury (1-01-01-010) amounting to ₱11,000,000.00 based on the
bank credit memo to recognize the deposit of the fund transfer; and (c) require
the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to, henceforth, conduct
regular reconciliation of the cashbooks with the subsidiary accounting records in
accordance with Sections 63(A.2) and 63.F of the Local Treasury Operations
Manual and pertinent provisions of the NGAS Manual.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare the
adjusting entry debiting Cash in Bank-Local Currency, Current Account (1-01-
02-010) and crediting Due to NGAS (2-02-01-050) amounting to ₱11,000,000.00.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
Municipal Accountant to update and maintain the subsidiary ledgers of the
general ledger accounts, conduct a thorough review of the affected accounts to
determine the other causes of the abnormal balances, initiate
corrections/adjustments, and thereafter come up with all accounts in their
normal balances to fairly present the financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Inventory
Committee to complete the physical count and direct the Accounting and
Property Units to reconcile the results of the physical count with the related
property and accounting records in accordance with the pertinent provisions of
COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, to ensure the accuracy of
the reported PPE balances in the financial statements. Any discrepancies noted
in the reconciliation shall be adjusted in accordance with Section 7.0 and 8.0 of
the Circular as a one-time cleansing of the PPE account balances.
The Municipal Treasurer informed the Audit Team that the physical count of
the Municipality’s movable properties was not completed because of the COVID-19
pandemic and a lack of personnel.

We recommended that Management enjoin the concerned department
heads to strictly comply with the accounting and reporting guidelines on the
local road asset management system and earnestly perform their respective
duties and responsibilities set forth under COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated
October 23, 2015.
We further recommended, and the Inventory Committee agreed, to
conduct an annual physical count of all LRN and the results thereof be reported
in the RPCLRN to be submitted to the Auditor and Accounting unit not later
than January 31 of each year. The RPCLRN shall be reconciled with the
accounting records, and the necessary adjusting entries shall be prepared before
the statement date.
The Municipal Accountant informed the Audit Team that the lack of
personnel or manpower to handle the matter preempted them from doing so.
However, initial actions were already taken by the Municipal Engineer. The
reconciliation of the accounts shall be made once the reports from the Inventory
Committee and the Municipal Engineer are completed.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the elected officials
who are not among those entitled to Hazard Pay as provided under Section 2 of
A.O. No. 26 and Section 3 of DBM Budget Circular No. 2020-1 dated March 24,
2020, to refund the amount of ₱126,000.00.
We further recommended that each respective Sanggunian appropriate
funds for hazard pay in accordance with Section 5 of A.O. No. 26 dated March
23, 2020, as amended by A.O. No. 43 dated June 1, 2021, and reimburse the
Trust Fund for the amount utilized for hazard pay, adequately supported with
the documentary requirements mentioned above to ascertain the propriety and
validity of the grant.
We furthermore recommended that Management use solely for COVID-
19 response operations the financial assistance given by the Provincial
Government, otherwise, return the unexpended balance to the Provincial
Government of Negros Oriental to be utilized for other equally important
government programs and projects.

We recommended that in the preparation of the Annual Budget, the Head
of the OSCA submit to the Municipal Budget Officer a request for allotment of
funds for the maintenance and operation of the OSCA showing the estimated
amount needed for each function, activity, or purpose consistent with the
Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs, as prescribed under COA Circular No.
2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the
abovementioned documentary requirements provided under Section 3.1 of COA
Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to support the fund transfers or
financial assistance extended to OSCA totaling ₱120,000.00.
The Municipal Budget Officer informed the Audit Team that the OSCA
submitted a budget proposal for its operation in CY 2021. Aside from the supplies,
fuel, and other maintenance and other operating expenses, the Municipality
appropriated a monthly financial assistance of ₱10,000.00 for the fare, snacks, and
other expenses during the monthly meetings of the OSCA.
Further, the Municipal Accountant commented in her written reply dated May
6, 2022, that the OSCA Head could no longer produce the receipts for their
purchases from the financial assistance granted by the Municipality because they
did not keep them. Management had already informed the OSCA of this audit
recommendation and that all parties have agreed to implement the same.
May we remind Management that financial assistance granted to OSCA for
maintenance and other operating expenses shall retain their character as public
funds. Therefore, it shall be disbursed following government existing laws, rules,
and regulations applicable to financial transactions.

We recommended that, henceforth, Management refrain from making
advance payments on government contracts unless specifically authorized by law
or regulations.
We further recommended that Management submit the original or
authenticated copies of the documents attached to the DV and the
abovementioned lacking documents required under Section 13.1 of COA
Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 for the Audit Team to properly
evaluate the propriety of the land purchase.
The Municipal Accountant commented in her written reply dated May 6,
2022, that Management had no intention of violating the rules and regulations, but in
the exigency of the need for a landfill, since the Municipality had already issued a
closure order, they did not have other recourse but to release the 50% down payment
so that the transfer of the title can be processed. However, the Municipality took hold
of the original title of the lot pending the transfer to protect the interest of the
government.
Management is enjoined to adhere to Section 88(1) of P.D. No. 1445 on the
prohibition against advance payment on government contracts and the documentary
requirements provided under Section 13.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June
14, 2012 for acquiring land.

We recommended that Management refrain from hiring consultants
whose functions can be performed by regularly appointed officials of existing
offices of the LGU as set forth in laws, rules and regulations or in their position
description form to avoid the unnecessary duplication of works/functions and
wastage of government funds.
We further recommended that, in the hiring of consultants, Management
should clearly define the consultants’ expected output in their individual
contract and require the latter to prepare their monthly accomplishment reports
vis-à-vis their respective definite expected output to establish the necessity of the
consultancy services they are employed for. Henceforth, payment of the services
rendered by medical consultants shall be properly supported with the
documentary requirements provided under Section 9.1.2 of COA Circular No.
2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.
In his written reply dated May 10, 2022, the Municipal Health Officer
informed the Audit Team that the hiring of the aforementioned physicians was
made due to the high demand of people seeking medical and surgical
consultations. A single physician will cause a delay in handling daily medical
consultations, thus causing some patients to queue for longer hours. The
recommended physician to population ratio by the Department of Health is one
physician to a population of 20,000 people. The Municipality has a population of
more than 24,000 people, which justifies the hiring of another rural health
physician.
Further, he informed us that there are instances when he undergoes training,
workshops, seminars, and meetings. Hence, he needs another physician for
medical and surgical consultations in his absence. Hiring of a rural health
physician or medical consultant is even more necessary as the country continues
to deal with the COVID-19 pandemic.
The MHO proposed the revision of the contract of services of the physicians’
duties and responsibilities as follows: (1) performs daily medical, surgical, and
obstetric consultations; (2) performs home visits and consultations; (3) gives
health teachings and promotes healthy lifestyle to patients; (4) screens patients for
COVID-19 vaccination; and (5) assists in the COVID-19 response and protection
program of the Municipality.
Based on the explanation of the MHO, the Audit Team understands the
necessity of hiring additional physicians in the Municipality. However, it should be
noted that they shall not perform the functions of the Municipal Health Officer nor
be designated to supervise health personnel, as stated in their Contract of Service,
pursuant to Section 7.0 of COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2, s. 2020, dated October
20, 2020.
Moreover, the Contract of Service and the Terms of Reference for the hiring
of physicians or medical consultants must clearly provide the expected output and
specific time within which to deliver the required output rather than stating it in
general terms in the contract as previously enumerated to provide a basis for
measuring the actual services rendered by them.

We recommended that Management submit a list of employees with
special achievements recognized by an outside group, indicating the amount of
monetary savings generated by their suggestions, inventions, superior
accomplishments, and other personal efforts in accordance with Section 6 of
CSC MC No. 1, s. 2001.
We further recommended that Management discontinue the use of their
savings for payment of monetary awards under the PRAISE program in the
absence of proof to establish that these savings were generated from employees’
suggestions, innovations, inventions, superior accomplishments, and other
personal efforts. Otherwise, they could be held liable for willful violation of
existing laws, rules, and regulations.
In her written reply dated April 5, 2022, the Municipal Budget Officer states
that the Municipality has an approved “Program on Awards and Incentives for
Excellence” from the Civil Service Commission. Hence, the Municipality granted a
monetary award to its employees based on the various awards and recognitions
enumerated above.
Further, the Municipal Treasurer informed in her written reply dated April 4,
2022, that she received two certificates of recognition from the Bureau of Local
Government Finance (BLGF), Department of Finance (DOF), Region VII for the
valuable contribution to the revenue generation program of the treasury operations in
surpassing the LGU collection targets. Since her achievement is a collective effort of
her staff in the Municipal Treasurer’s Office, she believed that it is reasonable that
her department be given a monetary award.
It is emphasized that monetary awards as provided under Section 6 of CSC
Memorandum Circular No. 01, s. 2001 dated January 26, 2001 “x x x shall only be
granted when the suggestions, inventions, superior accomplishments and other
personal efforts result in monetary savings which shall not exceed 20% of the
savings generated.” As there were no accomplishments or other reports documenting
the monetary savings generated by the awards and recognitions of the Municipality,
the grant monetary award under the Municipality’s PRAISE is improper.
Further, on the payment of monetary awards to the Office of the Municipal
Treasurer for surpassing the LGU’s collection targets, Section 18 of R.A. No. 7160,
otherwise known as “The Local Government Code of the Philippines,” on the power
to generate and apply resources, provides that “Local government units shall have the
power and authority to establish an organization that shall be responsible for the
efficient and effective implementation of their development plans, programs
objectives and priorities, to create their own sources of revenues and to levy taxes,
fees, and charges which shall accrue exclusive for their use and disposition and
which shall be retained by them; x x x; and to apply their resources and assets for
productive, developmental, or welfare purposes, in the exercise or furtherance of
their government or propriety powers and functions and thereby ensure their
development into self-reliant communities and active participants in the attainment
of national goals.” (Emphasis ours)
Hence, the collections that surpassed the Municipality’s targeted revenue must
not be granted as monetary awards, which benefits only certain individuals, but rather
to apply these resources for productive, developmental, or welfare purposes.

We recommended that Management desist from granting CEMA under
its PRAISE to employees designated to perform additional tasks, functions and
responsibilities. Likewise, we recommended that Management strictly adhere to
the provisions of CSC Memorandum Circular No. 1, series of 2001 and relevant
laws, rules, and regulations in the grant of monetary awards under the PRAISE.
In her written reply dated May 6, 2022, the Municipal Accountant commented
that the aforementioned employees are performing twice the necessary effort since
they were handling two offices. In effect the Municipality saved an ample amount
because instead of paying the regular basic pay for a Human Resource Officer and
General Services Officer, we paid them ₱5,000.00 per month for each employee. A
rate way below the basic pay for each position.
Management is enjoined to adhere to the applicable guidelines on the grant of
honoraria and RATA for filling temporary vacancy as provided under Section 6.0 of
the Local Budget Circular No. 62 dated July 29, 1996.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the suppliers to submit the original notarized OSS before or after award of contract but before payment is made and include a warranty clause in the contract or POs for procurements through emergency purchase in accordance with GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020. The LGU is implementing a “No Segregation, No Collection” policy.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the immediate refund in full of the amounts received in excess of what is authorized. observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the disbursement accounts to the Auditor within the reglementary period by ensuring that vouchers are paid only when supported with complete documentation, and prepare and submit the financial statements to the Auditor likewise within the prescribed period.

special achievements recognized by an outside group, indicating the amount of

their scarce resources by adhering to the limitations prescribed under COA

development projects.

We recommended that Management submit a list of employees with special achievements recognized by an outside group, indicating the amount of monetary savings generated by their suggestions, inventions, superior accomplishments and other personal efforts. Henceforth, adhere strictly to the provisions of CSC Memorandum Circular No. 1, series of 2001 in the granting of monetary awards under the PRAISE.
observations.managementAction: Management commented, in PRAISE Resolution No. 01, series of 2020, that the PRAISE Committee of the Municipality believed that the various awards and recognition received was the result of a concerted effort and cooperation of all officials and employees of the Municipality, thus, the granting of the said monetary awards was consistent with the provisions of CSC Memorandum Circular No. 01, series of 2001. However, the PRAISE Committee resolved to abide with the decision of the Audit Team and would refrain from granting monetary grants in the future.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to observe the prudent use of their scarce resources by adhering to the limitations prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2011-59 dated April 25, 2011, and, henceforth, conduct similar activities in the most economical manner possible to save funds for other development projects.
We further recommended and Management agreed to require the officials and employees concerned to refund the excessive traveling expenses totaling ₱57,090.00 and stop the practice of allowing national government personnel to charge their traveling expenses against the municipal funds.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to require all the PHWs to submit proof of actual exposure to specific health hazards and occupational risks, and documents required under Section 5.8 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to support their hazard pay.
observations.managementAction: Inquiry with the Municipal Budget Officer (MBO) disclosed that all PHWs of the Municipality are highly at risk of various communicable and infectious diseases as frontline healthcare providers. She further explained that they were given fixed rate of hazard pay for working at least 11 days in the Rural Health Unit in accordance with the provision of DBM and DOH Joint Circular, which states that “officials and employees who are rendering a combination or all of the following instances at least eleven working days in months shall be entitled to Hazard Pay.”

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that, henceforth, they will adhere strictly with the rates of hazard pay per month, dependent on the actual days of exposure to risks or danger, as prescribed under Section 3.3 of DBM-DOH Joint Circular No. 1 series of 2016 dated July 15, 2016, to ensure that health workers are justly compensated.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Budget Officer in her written reply dated June 3, 2020, which we received on June 23, 2020, informed the Audit Team that in CY 2020, the appropriation for hazard pay for health workers were adjusted accordingly as prescribed under Section 3.3 of DBM-DOH Joint Circular No. 1 series of 2016 dated July 15, 2016.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to stop charging the wages of JO and contract of service personnel, who performed administrative and maintenance works, to the 70% LDRRMF. Henceforth, utilize the fund for more worthy projects and programs that would strengthen the LGU’s capacity to avoid or mitigate the adverse effects of disaster or calamity.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization and include in the report all information required in Annex B of COA Circular No. 2012-002 and require the MDRRMO to submit the said report on or before the 15th day after the end of each month to the Office of the Auditor.

We recommended and Management agreed to stop charging wages of JO and contract of service workers to the 20% DF and henceforth, appropriate and utilize the Fund only for viable and sustainable development programs and projects enumerated under DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

We recommended that, henceforth, Management nominate only regular employees to attend conventions, seminars, trainings, conferences and such other activities to avoid incurring unnecessary expenses. Additionally, for trainings and seminars attended only by JOs or Contract of Service workers, we further recommended that Management require them to immediately cascade the knowledge and skills they learned from the training.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant in her written reply dated August 15, 2019 informed the Audit Team that her Office took note of the observation and thus would refrain from allowing Contract of Services and Job Order personnel from attending seminars and trainings. She further informed that the mentioned personnel were allowed that time since it was stipulated in their contract that they may be allowed to travel and claim travelling expenses when their job requires them to.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed that concerned government employees refund the excess amount of per diems claimed for their travels and, henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of the existing law, rules and regulations on travel.
We also recommended that government employees desist from claiming actual expenses incurred in official travel except those which are absolutely necessary under the circumstances duly supported with certification by the Municipal Mayor, the justification, terms and limitations of which should be indicated in the travel authority.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant in her written reply dated August 15, 2019 informed the Audit Team that her Office will facilitate in the refund of the excess travelling expenses and per diem and submit the photocopy of the official receipts. Likewise, she assured to provide a copy of the authority given by the LCE to some employees allowed to claim actual expenses during their travel.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management ensure that all Municipal employees with cash and property accountability are bonded as required under Sections 4.1 and 4.2 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 dated August 6, 2009 to safeguard the Municipality’s resources in case of loss.
observations.managementAction:
The ICO Municipal Treasurer in her written reply dated August 20, 2019 informed the Audit Team that she had overlooked the application of bond of the aforementioned Municipal employees because she handled two offices of the Municipality, the Municipal Assessor’s Office and Treasurer’s Office. observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Engineer, Municipal Agricultural Officer and Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Officer to fast track the implementation by taking appropriate action to address the cause of the delay and, henceforth, implement the projects on schedule. We further recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator closely monitor and evaluate the implementation of the different PPAs in accordance with the approved development plan as required of him under Section 476 (4) of RA 7160.
observations.reason: Some of these projects were still not implemented due to ROW problem per quarterly report on government PPAs in the 4th quarter of CY 2019. Likewise, the status of implementation of the livelihood projects and SAOB were not submitted to the Audit Team to validate compliance with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management comply with the budgetary requirement of one percent of the IRA for the implementation of PPAs of the LCPC. Henceforth, the council should ensure that target PPAs are implemented.
observations.reason: The Municipality allocated 1% of the IRA for LCPC budget which was mainstreamed to the budget of the Municipal Welfare and Development Office and Municipal Health Office. However, some PPAs are not properly chargeable to LCPC fund, thus, the budgetary requirement for LCPC was still not complied with.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Chairman of the LCPC see to it that the Council perform its functions in accordance with the DILG MC No. 2005-07 dated February 1, 2005.
observations.reason: The LCPC in the Municipality had not submitted the Records of Meetings and Accomplishment Report, which are indicators that the LCPC is operational or functional, hence, we could not review and validate whether it is fully operational.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the MSWMB ensure the implementation and compliance of the Solid Waste Management Plan of the Municipality and enjoin all the Barangays to actively participate in the waste reduction and segregation at source.
observations.managementAction:
 MENRO personnel are assigned at the controlled dumpsite and the Material Recovery and composting facility which serves as a final sorting/segregation station.
 The LGU is implementing a “No Segregation, No Collection” policy.
 The proposed Categorized Sanitary Landfill had passed the DENR-MGB, EMB and Provincial Assessor’s Office of Negros Oriental assessments. However, pending the release of funds from the Provincial Government of Negros Oriental, the lot was not yet procured.
 Continuous information-education-communication and public awareness campaigns on proper waste disposal was done by the MENRO.
 Initial steps for the safe closure and rehabilitation of the existing controlled dumpsite were started.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer remit the amount of withholding taxes to the BIR in accordance with the rules and regulations to avoid penalties and interest that might be imposed on late remittances.
observations.reason: The subsidiary records for “Due to BIR” account were still not submitted to the Audit Team, hence, we could not validate remittance of all taxes withheld.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the immediate refund in full of the amounts received in excess of what is authorized in DBM Budget Circular No. 2014-3 and discontinue the granting of PEI in excess of the allowable maximum rate of ₱5,000.00 per personnel, as provided for in Section 1.a of E.O. No. 80 dated July 20, 2012 to avoid overpayment.
observations.managementAction: Management already collected the CY 2014 refund of the excess payment through payroll deduction.
observations.reason: Refunds were made on installment basis through salary deduction.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the immediate refund in full of the amounts received in excess of what is authorized in DBM Budget Circular No. 2010-3. We also recommended that management direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to be vigilant and not to certify disbursements which go beyond the provisions of the law.
observations.managementAction: Management already collected the CY 2011 refund of the excess payment through payroll deduction.
observations.reason: Refunds were made on installment basis through salary deduction.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Accounting Office update the records of the LGU’s Property, Plant and Equipment. Likewise, the Municipal Treasurer‘s Office should maintain a reliable property cards/records. Furthermore, the LGU should conduct a physical inventory count at least once a year to determine which of their properties actually exist. A team composed of the General Services and Accounting Department personnel should conduct the physical inventory count and reconcile the book and property records. Accordingly, the Accounting Department shall effect the correcting entries, where applicable, when properly supported with documentation. Lastly, management should take action and drop the unserviceable properties from the inventory reports and dispose the same in accordance with the procedures outlined above.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee submitted the Inventory of Property, Plant and Equipment as of June 30, 2019 on August 22, 2019. Although the Inventory Committee has submitted the RCPPE as of June 30, 2019, reconciliation between the accounting records and the results of the count could not be made pending the submission of the property cards/subsidiary ledger.
observations.reason: RCPPE as of December 31, 2019 was not yet submitted by the Inventory Committee.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a. Management considers the disposal of its unserviceable properties. b. The Municipal Treasurer prepare the IIRUP with the appraisal of each item made by an appraisal committee for the purpose and have those items inspected and approved by the Regional Technical and Information Technology Services, COA Region VII. c. The Municipal Treasurer undertake the disposal of the unserviceable properties following the procedures and guidelines provided under Sections 502-505 of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual, Volume I. Furthermore, for a fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements, we require the municipal accountant to reclassify the unserviceable properties based on the IIRUP to Other Assets account and record the proceeds of the auction sale, if any.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory and Disposal Committee already prepared the IIRUP and Appraisal Report as of February 5, 2020 for unserviceable properties with net book and appraisal values of ₱63,860.00 and ₱57,230.00, respectively. The disposal of unserviceable properties was scheduled on April 16, 2020 but was postponed since the Province was declared under the Enhanced Community Quarantine (ECQ) from April 3 to April 30, 2020 to prevent the transmission of coronavirus disease 2019 (Covid-19).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require the Disbursing Officer to submit the lacking documents and henceforth, strictly comply with the documentary requirements enumerated under Section 1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-01 dated June 14, 2012.
observations.reason: The submission of the documentary requirements on the granting of cash advances for the payment of salaries, wages and allowances were still not being complied with.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant see to it that: (a) the grant of initial cash advance for the PCF is supported with authority by the Local Chief Executive and estimate of expenses; (b) replenishment of the cash advance is made only when disbursements reach at least 75%, or as the need requires, by submitting a replenishment voucher supported by PCVs and other documents duly summarized in a report of disbursements; and (c) the PCF is not used for regular expenses, such as, rentals, subscription, utilities and the like.
observations.reason: The documentary requirements on the establishment and maintenance of the Petty Cash Fund were still not observed. Likewise, it was observed that cash advances made by the Disbursing Officer for wages, salaries and allowances were erroneously charged to Petty Cash (1-01-01-020) and credited thereto upon liquidation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that, henceforth, Management desist from using the LDRRMF for expenses which are not disaster-related and strictly use the fund in accordance with the provisions of Republic Act No. 10121 and NDRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.
observations.reason: Expenses that were not disaster related were still charged to the LDRRMF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to comply with the budgetary requirement of one percent of the IRA for implementation of PPAs of the LCPC. Henceforth, the council should ensure that target PPAs are implemented.
observations.reason: The Municipality opted to mainstream its one percent budget for the Local Council Protection of Children (LCPC) contrary to Republic Act No. 9344 which requires that one percent of the IRA shall be allocated for LCPC programs and activities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to submit the documents to the municipal accountant, and the latter, to submit immediately to the auditor the Trial Balances (TBs), Financial Statements (FS) and other schedules for calendar year 2014 so that the LGU management may be timely informed of the municipality’s state of finances and to avoid the sanctions provided by law against erring officials.
observations.reason: The submission of accounts and the financial statements were still not submitted within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Property and Inspection Officer should desist from making advance payments in accordance with Item 4, Annex D of the Revised IRR of RA 9184. The Property and Inspection Officer must submit a written explanation on the discrepancy noted between the date the Acceptance and Inspection Report was signed by them and the actual dates of delivery of the materials per delivery receipts.
observations.reason: On a test basis, we post-audited some of the disbursement vouchers and noticed that there were payments made even prior to the delivery of the goods. The Municipal Treasurer prepared checks prior to the delivery as suppliers required COD.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer immediately collect the liquidated damages amounting to ₱140,664.02 less the amount of the performance security, if there is still any, from Earthworks Construction & Supply for the delayed deliveries of construction materials as provided in Paragraph 3.1, Annex D of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184.
observations.reason: The liquidated damages were still not collected by the Municipal Treasurer. Moreover, she assumed office as ICO-Municipal Treasurer only in September 2018 and had no knowledge about the transaction.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management should submit a copy of the Purchase Order/Job Orders/Contracts and each of all working days from execution to enable the timely review thereof.
observations.reason: Government contracts and purchase orders and their supporting documents were still not submitted to COA.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Municipal Assessor, as well as, the Municipal Treasurer to convene the Provincial Appraisal Committee to immediately act in determination of the value of the unrecorded lots. Also direct them to process the titling of the lots owned by the Municipality and the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate funds for the titling thereof. Likewise, instruct the Municipal Accountant to review the land account and record only in the books of accounts those lots that are owned by the municipality. Moreover, the value of the lots which will be determined by the Provincial Appraisal Committee shall also be recorded in the books of accounts to avoid understatement of assets.
observations.reason: The Municipal Assessor was not able to assess the value of these lots and the related documents still could not be located.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to validate the
balance per book and effect the immediate reconciliation of the Cash Local
Treasury general ledger and cashbook balance and take up the necessary
corrections in the books, where appropriate. Henceforth, reconcile the
cashbook balance with the accounting records at least every quarter in order
that differences between the two records can be adjusted right away to ensure
the fairness of the presentation of the Cash Local Treasury account in the
financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the
disbursement accounts to the Auditor within the prescribed period by ensuring
that vouchers are paid only when supported with complete documentation, and
prepare and submit the financial statements to the Auditor likewise within the
prescribed period.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to update the
monthly BRS for all bank accounts for CY 2018 for the immediate preparation
of correcting/adjusting entries for any discrepancies/errors or other reconciling
items noted to reflect the correct balance of the cash account in the financial
statements. We also recommended that, henceforth, the Municipal Accountant
submit the BRS to the Auditor within the prescribed timeframe.

We recommended and Management agreed to appropriate and utilize the
20% DF only for viable and sustainable development programs and projects
enumerated under DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1
dated February 22, 2017. We, however, recommended that these expenditures
may be charged to the General Fund Proper pursuant to existing rules and
regulations.

We recommended and the BAC Chairman agreed to submit the
aforementioned lacking documents including the checklist on the details of the
evaluation made by the BAC. We further recommended and Management
likewise agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to, henceforth, ensure the
completeness of supporting documents to the DVs covering payments of goods
procured thru competitive bidding in accordance with the pertinent provisions
of RA No. 9184 and Section 9.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14,
2012.

We recommended that the MSWDO prepare the PRs for the
procurement of food items based on the suggested cycle menu (Annex A of AO
No. 04, series of 2016). If there be any modification thereof, the MSWDO is
likewise required to attach in the DV the duly reviewed and approved menu by
the MNAO/Nutritionists of the Field Office so that purchases of ingredients
could be properly evaluated.

We recommended and Management agreed to optimally utilize the 70%
LDRRMF for its intended purpose and for expenditures that would strengthen
its capacity to avoid or mitigate the adverse effects of disasters or calamity.
Expenditures which are administrative in nature shall be properly charged
against the regular budget or funds of the Municipality.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare a
correcting entry to transfer the unexpended balances of the QRF and LDRRMF-
MOOE from the General Fund to the Trust Fund books. Likewise, we
recommended that the Municipal Treasurer coordinate with the Municipal
Accountant to ascertain the amount to be transferred to the Trust Fund in the
issuance of check.

We recommended and the Municipal Budget Officer agreed to,
henceforth, exclude the unexpended DRRMF-MOOE in the Continuing
Appropriations of the General Fund. Likewise, we recommended that it shall
only be utilized provided a revised AIP for new expenditures has been approved
by the Local Sangguniang and LCE.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to strictly
observe the perpetual inventory method in accounting for inventories as
provided under the NGAS Manual and maintain supplies ledger card.
We also recommended and the LDRRMO agreed to prepare the RSMI
supported by Requisition and Issue Slips for submission to the Municipal
Accountant to support the recording of expenses in the books of accounts.

We recommended and the LDRRMO agreed to (a) henceforth, maintain
stock cards to record the purchases and issuances of relief goods to monitor the
movement of goods stored in the stockroom; (b) submit the RDS to account for
the relief goods costing ₱49,788.30; and (c) refrain from distributing
prepositioned relief items to JO personnel who are not victims of calamity.

We recommended and Management agreed to require that the GAD
Focal Person to carefully identify and include in the GAD Plan and Budget only
PPAs that would address gender issues and concerns. Henceforth, stop the using
GAD funds for projects which are not gender-related.

We recommended and Management agreed to refrain from charging
expenditures not listed under Section 100.C of RA No. 7160 and DepEd-DBM-
DILG Joint Circular No. 1 series of 2017.

We recommended that Management strictly observe the regulations on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances pursuant to COA Circular No 97-002 dated February 10, 1997. observations.recommendation: We recommended that the accounting office update the records of the LGU‟s Property, Plant and Equipment. Likewise, the municipal treasurer‟s office should maintain reliable property cards/records. The two offices should make a periodic reconciliation of these records. Furthermore, the LGU should conduct a physical inventory count at least once a year to determine which of their properties are actually existing. A team composed of the General Services and Accounting Department personnel should conduct the physical inventory count and reconcile the book and property records. Accordingly, the Accounting Department shall effect the correcting entries, where applicable, when properly supported with documentation. Lastly, management should take action and drop the unserviceable properties from the inventory reports and dispose the same in accordance with the procedures outlined above.
observations.reason: Inventory count was not completed due to lack of manpower and time constraint.

observations.recommendation:

observations.recommendation:
a. Weak control was observed in the procurement of supplies and materials costing ₱886,162.87 which were not taken up as inventory but as outright expenses, contrary to Section 51 of the NGAS Manual, Volume I, thus financial statements were not fairly presented.

b. Require the Municipal Treasurer to prepare an inventory report by conducting a physical count of supplies and materials at the end of June and at the end of the year.

c. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to  properly maintain Supplies Ledger Card (SLC) for each type of commodity stock.

d. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain an inventory account for supplies and materials in stock and refrain from charging these as outright expenses.
observations.reason: Management still lacks personnel to handle the specific tasks.

observations.recommendation:
a. That management considers the disposal of its unserviceable properties.

b. That the municipal treasurer prepare the IIRUP with the appraisal of each item made by an appraisal committee for the purpose.

c. Have those items inspected and approved by the Regional Technical and Information Technology Services, COA Region VII.

d. That the municipal treasurer undertake the disposal of the unserviceable properties following the procedures and guidelines provided under Sections 502-505 of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual, Volume I.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management improve its GAD Plan and Budget by allocating 5% of the Annual Budget for Gender and Development. It has to incorporate those activities that address gender issues within the Municipality and those that are in accordance with GAD laws and guidelines, such as the Magna Carta of Women, Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of the PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA, the Philippine Plan for Gender Development, rules and regulations issued by the Philippine Commission on Women, etc. Moreover, management shall see to it that its GPB shall be submitted to the DILG and/or PCW for approval, in order to ensure that GAD issues and concerns are addressed.
observations.managementAction: The PPAs contained in the GAD Plan and Budget are mainstreamed in the regular programs of concerned offices.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management require the accountable officers with unliquidated cash advances to immediately submit the approved liquidation documents. Failure of the AO to liquidate his cash advances within the prescribed period shall constitute a valid cause for the withholding of his salary as provided for under paragraphs 9.2 and 9.3 of COA Circular 97-002 and the institution of other sanctions provided for under COA Circular No. 2012-004. Moreover, management should direct the municipal accountant to closely implement the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances, send final demand letters separately to each accountable officer to remind the AOs of their outstanding cash advances and their liquidation. Refrain from granting additional cash advance to officers and employees with outstanding cash advances.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer already demanded the collection of unliquidated cash advances.
observations.reason: There are still amounts that are left unsettled because some of the concerned personnel are already retired or deceased.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Inventory Committee include the count of supplies and materials when it conducts its physical count and the Accountant and Municipal Treasurer maintain required supplies ledger cards and stock cards, respectively, to comply with the prescribed procedures in the preparation of the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) so that utilization can be recorded appropriately in the books. Furthermore, the Municipal Accountant shall strictly follow the Perpetual Method of recording its inventory, as provided in the NGAS Manual and recognize inventory accounts for the above supplies immediately recorded as expenses.

observations.reason: The Property Custodian has not conducted the inventory of supplies and materials and failed to prepare the SSMI. She explained that she still needed to discuss this matter to the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant for her to be guided on the proper procedure and format of the report.
